summary: Jeff Atwood argues that the debate between C# and VB.NET is meaningless because both languages compile to the same .NET runtime and offer only superficial syntactic differences. He uses the Pepsi vs Coke analogy to illustrate that choosing between them isn't a real choice. The post expresses excitement about IronPython bringing a truly different language paradigm—dynamic typing and significant whitespace—to the .NET ecosystem. With the Visual Studio SDK finally providing IDE integration for IronPython, Atwood sees the original promise of .NET's language-agnostic runtime finally being realized.
key_points: The C# vs VB.NET debate is a red herring since both compile to the same runtime with only syntactic differences
Python represents a genuinely different paradigm with dynamic typing and indentation-based syntax
The .NET runtime's original promise was meaningful language choice, not just syntax variations
IronPython with Visual Studio IDE integration marks Python becoming a first-class .NET citizen
money_quotes: Choosing between C# and VB.NET isn't a meaningful choice. It's like choosing between .NET Pepsi and .NET Coke.
Either way, you're getting a cola flavored carbonated beverage.
If C# is Coke and VB.NET is Pepsi, then Python is Yoohoo.
Wasn't this the promise of the .NET runtime – that we'd have real, meaningful choice of language?
key_insight: True language choice on a runtime platform means offering fundamentally different programming paradigms, not just syntactic variations of the same underlying approach.
